name: Pixelfed
summary: Decentralized, open-source photo-sharing social network that can be used as an account-based alternative way to publish and share images across the Fediverse.

account.benefits: Users need an account on a Pixelfed instance to publish posts; limits and moderation depend on the chosen instance.

developer.notes: Pixelfed is open-source ActivityPub software. API and upload behavior are instance/application oriented rather than a simple anonymous file-host API.

allowed_file_types.notes: Pixelfed is photo/video social publishing software; upload limits and accepted media types are configured by each instance.

public_sharing.notes: Pixelfed posts and albums are designed for public/federated photo sharing, subject to post privacy and instance settings.

security.notes: Pixelfed instances run over HTTPS, but public/federated posts are not positioned as end-to-end encrypted file storage.

sources.label: Pixelfed.social about page
sources.notes: Official Pixelfed.social about page describes photo sharing, albums, comments, collections, and sign-up on an instance.

sources.label: Pixelfed GitHub repository
sources.notes: Official source repository describes Pixelfed as photo sharing for the fediverse and AGPL-licensed open-source software.

sources.label: Pixelfed docs prerequisites
sources.notes: Official docs show upload/file limits are configured at the instance/server level.

sources.label: FMHY Image Hosts
sources.notes: Seed list described Pixelfed as decentralized image sharing that requires sign-up; official sources support that classification.

primary_use.notes: Pixelfed is primarily a decentralized social network for photo/video posts rather than a normal anonymous file-host.

sharing_surface.value: Posts, albums, profiles, and ActivityPub federation
sharing_surface.notes: Sharing happens through posts, albums, profiles, and federated social distribution across instances.

max_file_size.notes: Upload size depends on the Pixelfed instance and its server configuration; official docs reference instance/server upload settings.

persistence_model.notes: Posts persist according to the selected instance's policies and the user's account/post deletion choices.

storage_model.notes: Storage is provided by each Pixelfed instance, not by a single central file-host pool.

bandwidth_model.notes: Bandwidth and delivery behavior depend on the user's selected instance and federated delivery paths.

name: SpectrShare
summary: Browser-to-browser P2P sharing method with no server storage, no account, no per-file size limit, WebRTC encryption, and optional in-browser video conversion.

account.benefits: No accounts are used; sharing is link/QR based while the host tab remains open.

developer.notes: SpectrShare is browser/P2P oriented and does not expose a public upload API. A previously cited GitHub repository link now returns 404, so open-source status is no longer treated as verified.

allowed_file_types.notes: All files can be downloaded; many image, audio, video, document, spreadsheet, email, ebook, and code formats can also be previewed in-browser.

public_sharing.notes: Sharing creates a link or QR code for recipients while the host tab remains open.

security.notes: The FAQ and technical notes describe direct WebRTC transfers with DTLS/end-to-end encryption and no server-side file storage.

sources.label: FAQ
sources.notes: FAQ describes P2P browser file sharing, no accounts, no server storage, no per-file size limit, supported preview formats, and WebRTC encryption.

sources.label: Technical details
sources.notes: Technical article explains direct WebRTC DataChannel transfer, streaming reads, ephemeral behavior, and DTLS encryption.

sources.label: FMHY video file hosts seed
sources.notes: Used only as the discovery seed for this batch; host facts are verified against first-party pages where available.

primary_use.notes: SpectrShare is a live transfer tool rather than a cloud file host.

sharing_surface.value: Share link or QR code while the host browser tab stays open
sharing_surface.notes: Recipients can download through the generated link/QR code during the live sharing session.

max_file_size.notes: The FAQ says there is no per-file size limit, with practical browser/device memory constraints for some fallback paths.

persistence_model.notes: Files exist for the transfer session only and are gone when the host tab closes.

storage_model.notes: Files are transferred directly between browsers and are not stored on SpectrShare servers.

bandwidth_model.notes: Throughput depends on host upload bandwidth, recipient download bandwidth, device capacity, and recipient count.

name: Telegram
summary: Cloud-based messaging service that can double as an ad hoc file-sharing method through chat attachments, channels, and public links.

account.benefits: A Telegram account is required. Shared files sync across devices and can be distributed through chats, channels, or public links.

developer.notes: Telegram publishes official file-upload and download API docs, but the consumer sharing workflow is chat-centric rather than a normal CLI file-host flow.

allowed_file_types.notes: Telegram says users can send and receive files of any type up to 2 GB each in regular cloud chats.

security.notes: Regular Telegram cloud chats are not end-to-end encrypted. Telegram treats Secret Chats as a separate, device-specific secure mode.

sources.label: Telegram FAQ
sources.notes: Official FAQ describing Telegram as cloud-based, supporting unlimited sharing of files up to 2 GB each, with Secret Chats as a separate privacy mode.

sources.label: Telegram 2 GB file sharing announcement
sources.notes: Official blog post announcing unlimited file sharing with files up to 2 GB each.

sources.label: Telegram downloads and attachments update
sources.notes: Official update saying users can send files of any type up to 2 GB each and access them from any device with unlimited cloud storage.

sources.label: Telegram file API docs
sources.notes: Official developer documentation for uploading and downloading files through Telegram APIs.

sources.label: Telegram Premium FAQ
sources.notes: Official Premium FAQ listing faster download speed as a paid feature and confirming unlimited cloud storage remains free.

sources.label: Telegram CDN docs
sources.notes: Official infrastructure documentation explaining that popular public files may be delivered from CDN data centers depending on regional traffic and popularity.

primary_use.notes: Telegram is primarily a cloud-based messaging platform that doubles as an ad hoc file-sharing method through conversations, channels, and public posts.

sharing_surface.notes: Shared files can move through private chats, groups, channels, and publicly reachable links.

max_file_size.notes: Telegram says users can send files of any type up to 2 GB each in regular cloud chats.

persistence_model.notes: Files in regular cloud chats remain until deleted by the user or chat participants. Secret Chats are separate and can self-destruct.

storage_model.notes: Telegram positions regular chats as unlimited cloud storage for shared files and media.

bandwidth_model.notes: Conditional: Telegram does not publish a simple file-host-style bandwidth quota. The official Premium FAQ makes faster download speed a paid feature, and Telegram's CDN docs show public-file delivery can depend on infrastructure path and popularity.

name: VKontakte
summary: Social network whose document attachments can also be used as an ad hoc file-sharing method.

account.benefits: A VK account is required to use document attachments as a sharing method inside the social network.

developer.notes: VK has an official developer platform, but this seed entry has not yet mapped a dedicated document-upload workflow in the public docs.

allowed_file_types.notes: Official VK document-upload documentation says document uploads may be any format except MP3 and executable files.

security.notes: VK runs over HTTPS, but the public official surfaces used here do not position document attachments as end-to-end encrypted file storage.

sources.label: VKontakte homepage
sources.notes: Official VK homepage confirming the service is an active social network product.

sources.label: VK developer portal
sources.notes: Official VK developer portal confirming an API surface exists.

sources.label: VK document upload documentation
sources.notes: Official VK developer documentation for document uploads states that documents may be any format except MP3 and executable files, with a file size of no more than 200 MB.

primary_use.notes: VK document attachments are secondary to the platform's main social-network workflow.

sharing_surface.value: Document attachments in chats and social surfaces
sharing_surface.notes: Files are shared through VK's account-based social and messaging surfaces rather than through a dedicated file-host workflow.

max_file_size.notes: Official VK document-upload documentation states that uploaded documents must be no more than 200 MB.

persistence_model.notes: Conditional: VK is a social-network document attachment surface rather than a standalone file host; the accessible official surfaces do not publish a fixed file-host-style retention window.

storage_model.notes: Conditional: storage is tied to VK account and document-attachment flows; the accessible official surfaces do not publish a separate file-host-style storage quota.

bandwidth_model.notes: Conditional: file access is delivered through VK's social and messaging surfaces; the accessible official surfaces do not publish a standalone file-host bandwidth cap.
